analogy: Think of "Cold War Era" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview The Berlin Wall, which had been built at the height of the Cold War and was its greatest symbol, was toppled by the people in 1989. This dramatic event was followed by an equally dramatic and historic chain of events that led to... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Cold War Era is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o verview The Berlin Wall, which had been built at the height of the Cold War and was its greatest symbol, was toppled by the people in 1989.

The text explains that this dramatic event was followed by an equally dramatic and historic chain of events that led to the collapse of the ‘second world’ and the end of the Cold War.

The text explains that germany, divided after the Second World War, was unified.

The text explains that one after another, the eight East European countries that were part of the Soviet bloc replaced their communist governments in response to mass demonstrations.

The text explains that the Soviet Union stood by as the Cold War began to end, not by military means but as a result of mass actions by ordinary men and women.

The text explains that eventually the Soviet Union itself disintegrated.

The text explains that in this chapter, we discuss the meaning, the causes and the consequences of the disintegration of the ‘second world’.

The text explains that we also discuss what happened to that part of the world after the collapse of communist regimes and how India relates to these countries now.

The text explains that chapter 1 The End of Bipolarity The Berlin Wall symbolised the division between the capitalist and the communist world.

The text explains that built in 1961 to separate East Berlin from West Berlin, this more than 150 kilometre long wall stood for 28 years and was finally broken by the people on 9 November 1989.

The text explains that this marked the unification of the two parts of Germany and the beginning of the end of the communist bloc.

The text explains that a section of the wall opened to allow free movement 3.

The text explains that the Berlin Wall as it stood before 1989 Credit: 1. and 2.

The text explains that frederik Ramm, www.remote.org/frederik/culture/berlin 3. www.cs.utah.edu

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 2 machinery production, and a transport sector that connected its remotest areas with efficiency.

The text explains that it had a domestic consumer industry that produced everything from pins to cars, though their quality did not match that of the Western capitalist countries.

The text explains that it had a complex communications network, vast energy resources including oil, iron and steel, Vladimir Lenin (1870-1924) Founder of the Bolshevik Communist party; leader of the Russian Revolution of 1917 and the founder-head of the USSR during the most difficult period following the revolution (1917-1924); a practitioner of Marxism and a source of inspiration for communists all over the world.

The text explains that t h e S o v i e t e c o n o m y used much of its resources in maintaining a nuclear and military arsenal and the development of its satellite states in Eastern Europe and within the Soviet system (the five Central Asian Republics in particular).

The text explains that shock therapy varied in intensity and speed amongst the former second world countries, but its direction and features were quite similar.

The text explains that source: https://www.unicef.org/hac2012/images/HAC2012_CEE-CIS_map_REVISED.gif Note: The boundaries and names shown and the designations used on this map do not imply official endorsement or acceptance by the United Nations.

The text explains that the free trade regime and foreign direct investment (FDI) were to be the main engines of change.

The text explains that each state from this bloc was now linked directly to the West and not to each other in the region.

The text explains that since the restructuring was carried out through market forces and not by government- directed industrial policies, it led to the virtual disappearance of entire industries.

The text explains that the reason for the revival for most of their economies was the export of natural resources like oil, natural gas and minerals.

The text explains that the Central Asian Republics are areas with vast hydrocarbon resources, which have brought them economic benefit.

The text explains that china has interests here because of the oil resources, and the Chinese have begun to settle around the borders and conduct trade.

The text explains that india too supported Soviet foreign policy in some crucial but indirect ways.

The text explains that russia is important for India’s nuclear energy plans a n d a s s i s t e d I n d i a ’ s s p a c e industry by giving, for example, the cryogenic rocket when India needed it.

The text explains that end of the ideological war between the US and USSR b.

The text explains that change in the balance of power in the world order d.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 14 E x e r c i s e s 5.

The text explains that the Soviet political system was based on ___________________ ideology. b. _________________ was the military alliance started by the USSR. c. ____________________ party dominated the Soviet Union’s political system. d. ______________________ initiated the reforms in the USSR in 1985. e.

The text explains that the fall of the ____________________ symbolised the end of the Cold War.

The text explains that mention any three features that distinguish the Soviet economy from that of a capitalist country like the US.

The text explains that what were the factors that forced Gorbachev to initiate the reforms in the USSR?

The text explains that what were the major consequences of the disintegration of the Soviet Union for countries like India?

The text explains that was this the best way to make a transition from communism to capitalism?

The text explains that write an essay for or against the following proposition: “With the disintegration of the second world, India should change its foreign policy and focus more on friendship with the US rather than with traditional friends like Russia”.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 1: Cold War Era

schemes.point: The Soviet state ensured a minimum standard of living for all citizens, and the government subsidised basic necessities including health, education, childcare and other welfare schemes. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch1 pg 1)

schemes.point: This was perhaps the biggest attempt in human history to abolish the institution of private property and consciously design a society based on principles of equality. (NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics Ch1 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LEADERS OF THE and SOVIET UNION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Cold War Era; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Cold War Era through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LEADERS OF THE and SOVIET UNION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
